key_metrics_output: Polymer type identification, extraction recovery (%), detection limits (MDL, LOQ, RDL), matrix interferences (false positives), quantification by Py-GC-MS/MS
abstract: This study assessed the efficacy of pyrolysis–gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (Py-GC-MS) for quantification of polymers in human blood. An extraction protocol was developed and validated to reduce matrix interferences of polyethylene (PE) and polyvinyl chloride (PVC). Extraction recoveries ranged 7–109%, with surface-modified polystyrene improving nanoparticle recoveries from 17 to 52%. Realistic detection limits were calculated accounting for matrix suppression and extraction recovery, up to 20 times higher than nominal limits in pure water. A pilot study of Australian population blood samples showed PE interferences persisted and no other polymers exceeded detection limits. The study concluded that Py-GC-MS is currently unsuitable for PE and PVC analysis in biological matrices due to interferences and nonspecific pyrolysis products, and estimated exposure concentrations needed approach the detection limits of the technique.

particle_size_range: plastics >300 nm can be identified confidently
Existing Particle Size Range: <5 mm (no lower bound)
key_metrics_output: Realistic detection limits were calculated for each polymer
identification and quantification of target polymers
Limits of quantification (LOQ) were calculated as the concentration of a peak with a signal:noise ratio of 10:1
abstract: assessed the appropriateness of pyrolysis−gas chromatography− mass spectrometry (Py-GC-MS) analysis for the quantification of a range of polymers in human blood
An extraction protocol that reduced matrix interferences (false positives) of polyethylene (PE) and polyvinyl chloride (PVC) was developed and validated
Extraction recoveries ranged 7−109%, although surface-modified polystyrene (carboxylated) increased nanoparticle recoveries from 17 to 52%
Py-GC-MS is currently not a suitable analysis method for PE and PVC in biological matrices due to the presence of interferences and nonspecific pyrolysis products
